metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the source label conflict is resolved: either extend `_spec()` to accept a `source` parameter, use a separate spec factory for structural specs, or explicitly update the held-out test's assertion — so the implementer has one unambiguous path.
evidence: Plan: 'the new/updated module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count MetricSpec entries carry source="structural"'
git_metrics.py line 258: `source="git"` hardcoded in _spec()
test_git_metrics_heldout.py line 93-100: asserts all REGISTRY specs from git_metrics have source="git"
findings.finding: The plan specifies `source="structural"` for the migrated module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count specs, but the existing `_spec()` helper in git_metrics.py hardcodes `source="git"` (line 258). These specs are currently registered through that helper with `source="git"`. The plan gives no direction on how to reconcile this conflict — whether to add a `source` parameter to `_spec()`, use a separate factory, or relocate the specs. Without resolution the implementer will either silently produce the wrong provenance label or break the held-out test that asserts `source="git"` for all git_metrics-registered specs.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section ("carry source='structural'") vs. git_metrics.py:258 (_spec helper, source="git")
scenarios: Implementer reuses _spec() → specs get source="git" not "structural", violating the plan's provenance contract and the registry.py seed entries' existing label.
Implementer invents an ad-hoc fix → held-out test breaks because it asserts source="git" for all git-metrics-registered specs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `source` keyword argument to `_spec()` (defaulting to `"git"` to preserve existing behavior) and call it with `source="structural"` for the two size specs; update the held-out test's assertion to check per-spec source rather than asserting all git_metrics specs share `source="git"`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the architectural path for `oversized_module_count`'s new compute: either (a) remove its seed entry from registry.py and have git_metrics.py's `register()` add it with the real compute, or (b) keep it in registry.py and import the compute callable from git_metrics.py with an explicit note on why that direction is safe.
evidence: registry.py lines 143-149: oversized_module_count is a seed entry with compute=_no_data_yet; no import from git_metrics.py exists
Plan: 'oversized_module_count is today a seed stub (_no_data_yet); this ticket gives it a real compute'
Files likely touched lists both git_metrics.py and registry.py but doesn't specify which owns the new compute
findings.finding: The plan says `oversized_module_count` (currently a seed stub in registry.py with `compute=_no_data_yet`) gets a real compute in this ticket, but doesn't specify WHERE the compute callable lives or how it reaches registry.py. registry.py is a declarative leaf with no imports from git_metrics.py. If the new compute is defined in git_metrics.py, registry.py must import from it (introducing a new cross-module dependency) or the spec must be replaced at registration time from git_metrics.py's register(). Neither path is spelled out, leaving the architectural placement ambiguous for the implementer.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics section ("oversized_module_count ... this ticket gives it a real compute") and Files likely touched (registry.py listed)
scenarios: Implementer puts compute in registry.py directly → violates the module's declared role as a pure declarative registry (no business logic).

Implementer puts compute in git_metrics.py and patches the REGISTRY list at register() time → works but is surprising and requires the seed entry to be removed from registry.py, which is not mentioned.
Implementer adds an import of git_metrics into registry.py → circular import risk since git_metrics imports from registry.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the oversized_module_count seed entry from registry.py and have git_metrics.py's register() add both module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count (with their real computes) to REGISTRY, consistent with how the other git_metrics specs are registered.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact shape of `AnalyzerResult.loc` (e.g., `dict[str, int]` keyed by file path, with `max_loc` as a separate top-level field `AnalyzerResult.max_loc`, or as a sub-object) and ensure the compute-path description in this plan uses the same attribute path as dep ticket 3b30's `AnalyzerResult` definition.
evidence: Plan says: 'compute reads `loc[<path>]` and `loc.max_loc`' (implying loc is itself an object with a max_loc attribute)
3b30 ticket says: '`AnalyzerResult` (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)' — loc is a plain optional field with no sub-attributes defined
18f9 ticket (scc adapter) uses `AnalyzerResult.loc.max_loc` in its own AC, creating a three-way inconsistency with no normative resolution in the plan under review
An executor implementing the compute() function cannot determine the correct attribute access chain from the plan text alone
findings.finding: The plan references `AnalyzerResult.loc` both as a per-file map (`loc[<path>]`) and as an object with a nested attribute (`loc.max_loc`), but the dep ticket [3b30] defines `AnalyzerResult` with top-level optional fields `loc/complexity/duplication` — none of which is itself an object with a `.max_loc` sub-attribute. The vocabulary of the `loc` field is never fully defined in the plan or the dep ticket, so the compute implementation cannot unambiguously resolve whether `max_loc` is `AnalyzerResult.loc.max_loc` (nested), `AnalyzerResult.max_loc` (top-level), or something else.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section: 'compute reads `loc[<path>]` and `loc.max_loc`'; dep ticket 3b30 AC: '`AnalyzerResult` (fields loc/complexity/duplication, each optional=None when unsupported)'
scenarios: Implementor writes `result.loc[path]` treating loc as a dict, but max_loc lookup fails because there is no .max_loc on a dict
Implementor writes `result.loc.max_loc` expecting a sub-object, but 3b30 ships loc as a plain dict/int
Tests pass with one shape but production code uses a different field path, causing silent AttributeError at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-line type annotation to the compute-path section, e.g.: '`AnalyzerResult.loc: dict[str, int]` (per-file LOC), `AnalyzerResult.max_loc: int` (top-level field produced by 18f9)' — or, if loc is a sub-object, define its fields explicitly. Confirm this matches 3b30's shipped dataclass.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the plan that the `oversized_module_count` seed entry in `registry.py` must be removed (or replaced) before or as part of wiring the real compute in `git_metrics.py`, and confirm that the `module_size_trend` seed stub is NOT removed (it stays as-is per per-metric semantics).
evidence: registry.py:143 seeds `oversized_module_count` with compute=_no_data_yet
git_metrics.py register() at line 267: `existing = {spec.id for spec in REGISTRY}` — if id already present, the new spec is skipped
Plan says 'this ticket gives it a real compute' but does not say to remove the registry.py seed entry first
ABSENCE: no instruction to delete the registry.py seed for oversized_module_count, no dedup resolution protocol specified
findings.finding: `oversized_module_count` exists as a seed stub in `registry.py` (line 143, `compute=_no_data_yet`) AND is named as a metric that gets a real compute in `git_metrics.py` via this ticket. No protocol is specified for resolving this collision: should the registry.py seed entry be removed, replaced, or does `register()` dedup by id? The existing `register()` dedup (git_metrics.py:267) skips ids already present — meaning the registry.py seed stub would BLOCK the new real compute from being registered.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics: '`oversized_module_count` is today a seed stub (`_no_data_yet`); this ticket gives it a real compute'; registry.py:142-149; git_metrics.py:267-277
scenarios: Implementor adds new compute to git_metrics.py register() but the seed stub in registry.py causes the dedup check to skip it — oversized_module_count silently remains as _no_data_yet and test_configured_positive fails
Implementor removes the registry.py seed to make room, but then module_size_trend (also a seed) is accidentally removed too
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Files likely touched section or per-metric semantics: 'Remove the `oversized_module_count` MetricSpec entry from `registry.py` REGISTRY list; it will be registered by `git_metrics.register()` with the real compute. The `module_size_trend` seed entry stays.'

findings.finding: The plan says 'oversized_module_count is today a seed stub (_no_data_yet); this ticket gives it a real compute' but does not specify the mechanism for replacing that seed. The current register() in git_metrics.py skips any id already present in REGISTRY (idempotent-on-id). Because registry.py already seeds 'oversized_module_count' with _no_data_yet, a naïve new _spec(..., 'oversized_module_count', ...) added to register() will be silently dropped — the stub will persist and AC2 will fail.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) section; registry.py lines 142–149; git_metrics.py register() lines 264–277
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `oversized_module_count` MetricSpec from the REGISTRY seed list in registry.py and add it to git_metrics.register() alongside module_size_distribution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan how module_size_distribution's MetricSpec will acquire source="structural" given that the _spec() helper hardcodes source="git" — either by creating a separate builder, extending _spec() with a source parameter, or registering the spec directly.
evidence: Plan: 'the new/updated module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count MetricSpec entries carry source="structural"'
git_metrics.py:254-261: _spec() builder hardcodes source="git" for all specs
registry.py:144: oversized_module_count seed spec already has source="structural" (set directly, not via _spec())
git_metrics.py:269: module_size_distribution currently registered via _spec() with source="git"
findings.finding: The plan states the updated `module_size_distribution` and `oversized_module_count` specs will carry `source="structural"`, but the existing `_spec()` builder in `git_metrics.py` hardcodes `source="git"` for all specs it creates. The plan does not specify how `module_size_distribution` will switch source labels (the current `_spec()` helper cannot produce `source="structural"`).
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism (G6/T8/E4) section: 'carry source="structural"'
scenarios: If the implementer uses the existing _spec() helper to register the migrated module_size_distribution, it will carry source="git" instead of the specified source="structural", causing test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve to fail.
The provenance AC check (and authoritative-series classification) will be incorrect.
findings.suggested_fix: Either add a `source` parameter to `_spec()` defaulting to `"git"`, or register `module_size_distribution` and `oversized_module_count` directly as `MetricSpec(...)` instances with `source="structural"` rather than going through `_spec()`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Name the new context attribute that carries the `AnalyzerResult` in the Compute-path section (e.g. add `ctx.analyzer_result: AnalyzerResult` alongside `ctx.size_cap` and `ctx.size_near_fraction`).
evidence: 'The existing `_spec` context adapter (which passes only `repo_root/since/until`) is EXTENDED to also resolve and pass the analyzer result.'
'ctx attributes: ctx.size_cap and ctx.size_near_fraction (resolved from the `[code_health]` section).' — AnalyzerResult attribute name is absent.
findings.finding: The plan says the existing `_spec` context adapter 'is EXTENDED to also resolve and pass the analyzer result' but never names the new attribute on the context object that carries the AnalyzerResult to the compute functions. `ctx.size_cap` and `ctx.size_near_fraction` are named, but the AnalyzerResult accessor is not — an executor must invent a name.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section — '…the _spec context adapter… is EXTENDED to also resolve and pass the analyzer result'
scenarios: An executor writing the context adapter must choose an attribute name (e.g. ctx.analyzer_result vs ctx.loc_data vs ctx.loc) and the compute functions must agree; if they diverge the unit tests will pass on one name but the runtime wiring uses another.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one line to the Compute-path mechanism section: `ctx.analyzer_result: AnalyzerResult` — the object returned by `resolve_analyzer(ctx.repo_root, cfg)` and passed through the extended `_spec` adapter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether oversized_module_count's compute moves into git_metrics.register() (and the seed stub is removed from registry.py's REGISTRY list) or stays in registry.py with an explicit import strategy that avoids circular dependency.
evidence: registry.py:143-149: MetricSpec(id='oversized_module_count', ..., compute=_no_data_yet)
git_metrics.py:264-277: register() does NOT include oversized_module_count
Plan: 'oversized_module_count is today a seed stub (_no_data_yet); this ticket gives it a real compute'
findings.finding: The oversized_module_count seed stub lives in registry.py's REGISTRY list (line 143, compute=_no_data_yet), not in git_metrics.py's register(). The plan gives it a 'real compute' from AnalyzerResult.loc, but does not specify whether it migrates to git_metrics.register() (introducing a dependency on analyzer.py) or stays in-place in registry.py (requiring registry.py to import from git_metrics or analyzers/, risking a circular import). This ambiguity is load-bearing for the implementer.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics / src/rebar/metrics/registry.py:143
scenarios: Implementer wires the compute in registry.py directly, creating a circular import when registry.py imports AnalyzerResult from analyzer.py (which imports registry.py symbols).
Implementer moves the spec to git_metrics.register(), unaware the seed stub also needs removal from registry.py's REGISTRY list, causing a duplicate-id collision.
findings.suggested_fix: Move the spec to git_metrics.register() (parallel to module_size_distribution) and remove the seed stub from registry.py's REGISTRY list. Note this in 'Files likely touched' for registry.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the transition strategy for `_NEAR_CAP_FRACTION`: specify whether the constant becomes a default fallback when `ctx.size_near_fraction` is absent, is removed entirely (requiring all call sites to go through ctx), or whether the standalone function signature is updated to accept it as a parameter.
evidence: git_metrics.py line 36: `_NEAR_CAP_FRACTION = 0.10` hardcoded
git_metrics.py line 92: `near_threshold = cap * (1 - _NEAR_CAP_FRACTION)` — uses the constant directly in the function body
Plan: 'ctx.size_cap and ctx.size_near_fraction (resolved from the [code_health] section)'
findings.finding: The plan states that `size_near_fraction` comes from `ctx` (resolved from the `[code_health]` config section), but the current codebase has it hardcoded as a module-level constant `_NEAR_CAP_FRACTION = 0.10` in git_metrics.py (line 36) used directly in `module_size_distribution()`. The migration mechanism for replacing this constant with a config-driven value on the context object is not described, leaving the implementer to guess whether to remove the constant, keep it as a default fallback, or require it on ctx.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section ("ctx.size_cap and ctx.size_near_fraction") vs. git_metrics.py:36 and git_metrics.py:92
scenarios: Implementer removes _NEAR_CAP_FRACTION and requires ctx.size_near_fraction → existing tests that call module_size_distribution(repo_root) directly (not through ctx) break.
Implementer keeps _NEAR_CAP_FRACTION as a fallback but the plan's AC does not test the config-driven fraction path, so the migration is incomplete.

evidence: 2 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test) for the 'configured but analyzer fails at runtime' path (e.g., scc unavailable or resolve_analyzer raises), verifying it returns Unavailable with no leaked exception rather than propagating an error.
evidence: AC 'Unconfigured analyzer -> Unavailable, NOT {count:0,max_loc:0}' covers only the 'not configured' case
'resolve_analyzer ... obtains the AnalyzerResult' — no mention of exception handling if resolve_analyzer raises
No AC or test covers 'configured + scc failure' or 'configured + malformed result' paths
'AnalyzerResult.loc is the per-file LOC map + max_loc PRODUCED by the scc adapter' — scc is a subprocess dependency
findings.finding: The plan specifies the 'unconfigured analyzer → Unavailable' path but does not address the failure mode when the analyzer IS configured but fails at runtime (e.g., scc subprocess crash, missing binary, malformed AnalyzerResult), leaving the reliability behavior and user-facing signal for this case unspecified and untested.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria / Compute-path mechanism / Per-metric post-migration semantics
scenarios: scc binary is installed but crashes on a large repo; resolve_analyzer raises; the metric compute propagates an unhandled exception instead of returning Unavailable.
A CI environment has the [code_health] config but scc is missing; the metric returns a noisy traceback rather than a clean Unavailable.
findings.suggested_fix: In the compute path for module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count, wrap the resolve_analyzer call in a try/except that catches all analyzer errors and returns MetricResult(status=Unavailable, ...) with a logged warning — and add a test `test_configured_analyzer_runtime_failure` that injects a mock resolve_analyzer that raises and asserts the metric returns Unavailable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Move the embedded `- [ ]` criterion from 'Compute-path mechanism' into the main AC block (as AC #7), and add `test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve` to the 'Files likely touched' test list.
evidence: The main AC block contains exactly 6 numbered criteria; this item is a 7th buried in the body.
'test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve' does not appear in the AC block, the Grounding section, or the 'Files likely touched' list.

It names a specific test (`test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve`) that appears nowhere in the main AC list, 'Files likely touched', or the Grounding section — making it an orphan deliverable with no corresponding AC coverage and an unnamed test file to create.

scenarios: Implementer treats the embedded item as informational rather than mandatory and skips writing `test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve`; provenance is unverified.
Reviewer signs off on all 6 ACs without noticing the 7th requirement, shipping unverified provenance.

PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics.py::test_size_specs_provenance_and_resolve`. Remove the `- [ ]` bullet from the Compute-path body section (leave the prose description).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one boundary-case data point to `test_configured_positive` (or a companion test) covering: a file with LOC exactly at `size_cap` (clarify and assert whether it counts as oversized), and a file within the `size_near_fraction` zone (assert its distribution bucket), so the boundary semantics are specified by the test rather than locked in by accident.
evidence: 'files at 100 and 850, cap 800 -> over_cap_count==1, max_loc==850' — only one non-trivial data point, no boundary case

'size_cap/size_near_fraction, which come from the [code_health] config section' — size_near_fraction is mentioned as a config input to compute but its effect on counts/distribution is not tested at any boundary
ABSENCE: no test case for file LOC == cap, LOC == cap-1, or LOC == cap * (1 - size_near_fraction)
findings.finding: The plan's sole positive test (`test_configured_positive`) uses files at 100 and 850 with cap 800, but never probes the at-cap boundary (file LOC == 800) nor the near-cap boundary implied by `size_near_fraction`. The boundary semantics of `oversized_module_count` (is exactly-at-cap counted as oversized? how does `size_near_fraction` affect counts or distribution buckets?) are unspecified and untested, risking an off-by-one that gets locked in as correct by the test.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `test_configured_positive` / Compute-path mechanism section (`size_near_fraction`)
scenarios: A file exactly at the cap is incorrectly counted as oversized (or not), and the test passes both ways
The near-fraction boundary changes distribution bucket assignment but is never exercised, so a bucketing bug goes undetected
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the synthetic AnalyzerResult fixture to include a third file at LOC==800 (exactly at cap) and a fourth at LOC==800*(1-size_near_fraction), then add explicit assertions on over_cap_count and distribution bucket membership for each. Document the intended boundary convention (e.g., strictly-greater-than means cap==800 file is NOT oversized) in a comment above the assertions.

AC5: 'PROVE: pytest -q tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py::test_foreign_repo_honest_unavailable'

scenarios: An executor following 'Files likely touched' as a work checklist skips tests/interfaces/test_metrics_cli.py; the proving commands in AC3 and AC5 then fail with a collection error, blocking sign-off.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `cap_change_events` deregistration (e.g., a revert commit procedure, or a temporary compat shim that returns Unavailable before full removal), and document it in the plan.
evidence: Plan states: 'DEREGISTER the spec from the portable registry (remove from `register()`), documented in the close note.'
No rollback path, feature flag, or expand-contract sequence is described anywhere in the plan.
T4 requires: 'The remedy is an explicit rollback/back-out plan or expand-contract — acknowledgement alone is insufficient.'
findings.finding: The `cap_change_events` deregistration is described as a destructive, irreversible removal from the portable registry, but the plan provides no rollback path or expand-contract sequencing — only documentation in the close note.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics: `cap_change_events` bullet; AC #3
scenarios: A downstream consumer (e.g., a dashboard or alerting rule) queries `cap_change_events` after deployment and receives an error or silent omission, with no ability to revert to the registered state without a new deployment.
The deregistration is merged and a dependent system breaks; the team has no documented procedure to revert.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the breaking behavior change (zeros→Unavailable) for unconfigured repos in the plan, and add either a rollback procedure or an explicit statement that no consumers depend on the zero-valued response (with evidence).
evidence: Plan states goal: 'a non-Python client gets honest Unavailable rather than fabricated zeros' — explicitly changes the return value shape for the unconfigured path.
No expand-contract sequence (e.g., emit both old and new shape temporarily) or rollback path is described.
AC #5 verifies the new behavior but provides no migration path for existing consumers expecting `{count:0,max_loc:0}`.
findings.finding: The behavior change from returning fabricated zeros to returning Unavailable for `module_size_distribution` and `oversized_module_count` on unconfigured/non-Python repos is a breaking interface change for consumers of these metrics, but no rollback path or expand-contract sequence is described.
findings.location: Problem statement ('honest Unavailable rather than fabricated zeros'); AC #5 (`test_foreign_repo_honest_unavailable`)
scenarios: A consumer that treats `{count:0,max_loc:0}` as a valid 'no modules oversized' signal now receives Unavailable and interprets it as a system error or renders a broken UI.
Rollback requires reverting the entire ticket, not a targeted configuration change.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the extended `_spec` context adapter contract (now carrying `AnalyzerResult` alongside `repo_root/since/until`) in AGENTS.md, a design doc, or an inline architectural note, so future metric authors know how to access the analyzer from `ctx`.
evidence: 'The existing _spec context adapter (which passes only repo_root/since/until) is EXTENDED to also resolve and pass the analyzer result'
No mention of ADR, AGENTS.md, or design doc update anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The extension of the `_spec` context adapter to carry an `AnalyzerResult` is a novel cross-component architectural pattern/contract, but the plan mentions no ADR, AGENTS.md entry, or design-doc update to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section / src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py (_spec adapter)
scenarios: A future developer adding a new metric spec does not know the context adapter was extended and how to access the analyzer result from ctx, leading to re-invention or bypassing the pattern.
The contract between the metrics layer and the analyzer component is undocumented, making it hard to evolve safely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a section to AGENTS.md (or a docstring on the `_spec` adapter function) describing: (1) what ctx attributes are available (repo_root, since, until, size_cap, size_near_fraction, analyzer_result), (2) how the analyzer is resolved (via resolve_analyzer + [code_health] config), and (3) how to add a new metric spec that uses the analyzer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that tickets `rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc` and `rebar:18f9-5b75-b909-408c` are accessible in the tracker and that their APIs (`resolve_analyzer`, `AnalyzerResult.loc`, `AnalyzerResult.loc.max_loc`) match the exact shapes the compute path assumes.

findings.finding: The plan's core compute-path relies on `resolve_analyzer` (attributed to `[rebar:3b30-2168-ba16-41fc]`) and `AnalyzerResult` with a `.loc` per-file map (attributed to `[rebar:18f9-5b75-b909-408c]`), but both prerequisite tickets return NOT FOUND from the ticket tracker and neither symbol exists anywhere in the codebase. The cited-prerequisite escape requires affirmative confirmation from `show_ticket`; failure to retrieve the tickets leaves these assertions unbacked.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section; Grounding (E4/G1G2) section; Per-metric post-migration semantics
scenarios: The implementer cannot verify what interface resolve_analyzer exposes or what shape AnalyzerResult.loc takes, risking mis-integration.
If these prerequisites are not yet merged, the plan's compute-path cannot be implemented as written.

Plan: 'ctx.size_cap and ctx.size_near_fraction (resolved from the [code_health] section)'

findings.finding: The plan asserts that `ctx.size_cap` and `ctx.size_near_fraction` are resolved from a `[code_health]` config section created by prerequisite `[rebar:e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb]`, but that ticket returns NOT FOUND from the tracker and neither attribute nor the config section exists anywhere in the codebase. This leaves the config-resolution path for the compute unverifiable.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics section; Compute-path mechanism section
scenarios: If the [code_health] config section is not yet present when implementation begins, the implementer has no concrete schema to code against.
If ctx.size_cap / ctx.size_near_fraction are not populated by the config infrastructure, the compute silently uses wrong or missing cap values.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly note that `test_git_metrics_heldout.py::test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels` must remove `cap_change_events` from the `named` set (line 98) and update the header docstring (line 6) to reflect deregistration.

Plan: 'cap_change_events: DEREGISTER the spec from the portable registry (remove from register())'
Plan names test_git_metrics_heldout.py as a file to update but does not identify this specific assertion conflict
findings.finding: test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98 explicitly asserts that 'cap_change_events' belongs to the named set of registered metric IDs (named = {'module_size_distribution', 'churn', 'cap_change_events', 'refactor_to_addition_ratio'}). Deregistering cap_change_events will cause test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels to fail, yet the plan does not enumerate this specific assertion as requiring deletion/update.
findings.location: Files likely touched / tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:98
scenarios: Implementer updates test_git_metrics_heldout.py for cap_change_events removal but overlooks test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels, leaving a broken assertion that blocks CI.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 'Files likely touched': 'tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py — remove cap_change_events from the named set in test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels and update the module docstring.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note that test_git_metrics_heldout.py::test_git_metrics_registered_with_labels must be updated if the size specs change from source='git' to source='structural' (or confirm they stay 'git' and correct the plan's 'Compute-path mechanism' section).
evidence: Plan: 'the new/updated module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count MetricSpec entries carry source="structural"'
git_metrics.py:257: source="git" (in _spec() helper)
test_git_metrics_heldout.py:93: git_specs = {s.id: s for s in REGISTRY if s.source == "git"}
findings.finding: The Compute-path mechanism section states the migrated size specs carry source='structural', but the _spec() helper in git_metrics.py hardcodes source='git' (line 257), and test_git_metrics_heldout.py filters for git specs by source=='git' (line 93). If module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count switch to source='structural', they will silently disappear from that test's filter — a consumer conflict not enumerated by the plan.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section / src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py:257 / tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:93
scenarios: After migration, module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count are registered with source='structural'; the existing test filter s.source=='git' silently excludes them, giving a false green while the provenance change goes untested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the cap_change_events function is removed from git_metrics.py or retained (stub/deprecated), and update the 'Files likely touched' update note for test_git_metrics_heldout.py to cover the direct-import tests at lines 17, 61–79.
evidence: test_git_metrics_heldout.py:17: from rebar.metrics.git_metrics import cap_change_events, ...
Plan: 'DEREGISTER the spec from the portable registry (remove from register())'
Plan does not say 'remove the function' — only remove from register()
findings.finding: test_git_metrics_heldout.py line 17 imports cap_change_events directly from rebar.metrics.git_metrics. The plan says to deregister cap_change_events from register() but does not state whether the function itself is removed from the module. If the function is deleted, this direct import (and the tests at lines 61–79) will break; if the function is retained but merely deregistered, the plan's AC grep test ('! grep -Eq ...') would still pass since the function name isn't being grepped for.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (cap_change_events) / tests/unit/metrics/test_git_metrics_heldout.py:17
scenarios: If the function is removed: the direct import at line 17 raises ImportError, breaking test_cap_change_events and test_over_cap_count_uses_the_single_sourced_cap.
If the function is kept: the heldout test still exercises old behavior (scanning src/rebar + reading module-size-limit.txt), leaving tests that contradict the migration goal green but misleading.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify, in the compute-path or AC section, the exact fallback rule when `resolve_analyzer` returns None: either (a) compute returns `None` (accepting the generic reason string from `evaluate()`), or (b) compute returns `Unavailable(reason='no analyzer configured for python', accruing_since=_ACCRUING_SINCE)` directly — and confirm which `reason` string `test_foreign_repo_honest_unavailable` asserts.
evidence: registry.py evaluate() converts None return -> Unavailable(reason=f"no data has accrued for '{spec.id}' yet", accruing_since=spec.accruing_since)
3b30 ticket's fallback helper returns Unavailable(reason='no analyzer configured for <lang>', accruing_since=<ts>) — a different reason string
Plan does not specify which Unavailable path is taken, nor which reason string / accruing_since timestamp the compute should produce
AC test `test_foreign_repo_honest_unavailable` would exercise this but its expected output is not specified in the plan
findings.finding: The fallback decision rule when `resolve_analyzer` returns `None` (no analyzer configured) inside the new compute path is not co-located with the adapter extension protocol. The plan states the goal ('Unavailable rather than fabricated zeros') but does not specify the concrete rule: does the adapted compute() return `None` (delegating to the registry's generic `evaluate()` path) or return an `Unavailable` instance directly? These produce different `reason` strings and different `accruing_since` values.
findings.location: Compute-path mechanism section; AC: 'Unconfigured analyzer -> Unavailable, NOT `{count:0,max_loc:0}`'
scenarios: Compute returns None; registry produces reason='no data has accrued...' but the test asserts reason='no analyzer configured...' — test fails
Compute returns Unavailable directly; evaluate() receives a non-None truthy value and wraps it as a MetricValue, producing a garbled output

findings.finding: The plan asserts it will compute `over_cap_count` by iterating per-file LOC from `AnalyzerResult.loc`, but no prerequisite ticket specifies the attribute name(s) for the per-file collection within `AnalyzerResult.loc`. Ticket 3b30 defines `loc` as a field (optional=None when unsupported) without naming its sub-structure; ticket 18f9 says it carries 'per-file LOC + max' but only names `max_loc` explicitly (in `.loc.max_loc`). The plan's AC test (`test_configured_positive`) requires iterating individual file sizes (100, 850) against a cap — a computation that requires a per-file attribute name that is never cited.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — `module_size_distribution`/`oversized_module_count` bullet; AC line: `test_configured_positive`

findings.finding: The plan states that size_cap and size_near_fraction come 'from ctx' and that AnalyzerResult.loc is consumed 'read from ctx', but never specifies the attribute names by which the new compute() reads these values off the context object. The existing _spec() adapter only pulls repo_root/since/until from ctx (git_metrics.py:245–252). An implementer must invent attribute names, risking mismatch with whatever the prerequisite ticket (e7a0) actually puts on the context.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) section; git_metrics.py _spec() adapter lines 242–252

findings.finding: The ctx attribute names for size_cap and size_near_fraction are never specified: the plan says they 'come from the [code_health] config section … resolved onto the context object (read from ctx)', but never names the ctx attribute(s) the new compute adapter must call (e.g. ctx.size_cap, ctx.code_health.size_cap, ctx.config.code_health.size_cap). The [code_health] section is also absent from the live _config_schema.py and _SECTION_CLASSES, and prerequisite e7a0 cannot be loaded.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count bullet; Grounding section (E4/G1G2)

findings.finding: The plan does not specify how AnalyzerResult reaches the compute callback. The existing _spec adapter reads only repo_root/since/until from ctx; the new compute must either (a) call resolve_analyzer(lang)(ctx.repo_root) inline, (b) read a pre-computed AnalyzerResult from ctx, or (c) receive the analyzer via ctx. These options have different ordering constraints (who runs the analyzer, when, and with which error-handling contract), and the plan text leaves the implementer to invent this junction without guidance.
findings.location: Mechanism section — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count; Plan corrections (round 4) SIZE CAP SOURCE
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Mechanism section: 'The compute callable for module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count calls resolve_analyzer("python") to obtain the Analyzer, then invokes analyzer.analyze(ctx.repo_root) inline; if resolve_analyzer returns None or analyze() raises/returns Unavailable, the compute returns None (yielding Unavailable from registry.evaluate()). This keeps compute callbacks self-contained, consistent with the existing _spec adapter pattern.'

findings.finding: No existing mechanism delivers an AnalyzerResult to a MetricSpec compute callback. The current _spec adapter in git_metrics.py reads only repo_root/since/until from ctx (lines 245–251); no AnalyzerResult field exists on any context object in the codebase. The plan states readers 'compute from AnalyzerResult.loc' but does not specify how AnalyzerResult is obtained inside the compute lambda — whether by calling a resolver, reading a ctx attribute, or something else.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6); Files likely touched: src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'The metrics context object will expose `ctx.analyzer_result: AnalyzerResult | None`; when None (unconfigured or non-Python client), the compute callback returns None → Unavailable. The CLI populates this attribute by calling `rebar.metrics.analyzer.analyze(ctx.repo_root)` before dispatch.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts computing `max_loc` and `over_cap_count` from `AnalyzerResult.loc`, but `AnalyzerResult` is defined in open prerequisite ticket 3b30 whose `analyzer.py` is confirmed absent from the codebase. The concrete shape of `.loc` (e.g., `dict[str, int]` keyed by file path, a dataclass field, a list of per-file structs) is never specified in this plan. The AC test pins `over_cap_count==1, max_loc==850` without stating how the reader traverses `.loc` to derive those values — an unverified capability assertion that the implementer cannot resolve until 3b30 merges.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) / AC line: 'Configured positive path'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to the 'Per-metric post-migration semantics' section: e.g., 'AnalyzerResult.loc is a `dict[str, int]` mapping module path to LOC count; `max_loc = max(loc.values())` and `over_cap_count = sum(1 for v in loc.values() if v > size_cap)`.' If 3b30 uses a different shape, sync this plan with 3b30's AC before implementation.

findings.finding: AnalyzerResult.loc field structure is referenced but never defined: the plan says compute max_loc and over_cap_count from AnalyzerResult.loc, but neither the plan nor any reachable prerequisite (ticket 3b30 not found, analyzer.py absent from repo) specifies whether .loc is a dict {path→int}, a list, or a pre-computed object. An implementer cannot write the compute without guessing the iteration protocol.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count bullet; AC bullet 2 (test_configured_positive)

findings.finding: AC1's grep prove command verifies that the hardcoded path strings are absent, but no criterion verifies that the `cap_change_events` *computation logic* (e.g., `_read_cap`, `_LIMIT_FILE`, the git-log scan at line 167) is actually removed from `git_metrics.py` — only that it is not registered and the string literals are gone. Dead computation code for a deregistered metric is an incomplete migration.
findings.location: AC1 (grep prove) vs. Grounding section (confirmed locations)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1's grep to also check: `! grep -Eq '_read_cap|_LIMIT_FILE|cap_change_events' src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py src/rebar/metrics/registry.py`

findings.finding: AnalyzerResult (from prerequisite 3b30-2168-ba16-41fc) does not exist in the codebase — no such class, module, or file is present under src/rebar — and the prerequisite ticket cannot be fetched. The plan's core compute logic for module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count depends entirely on AnalyzerResult.loc having per-file LOC data in a shape the reader can iterate, but this shape is asserted without any verifiable specification.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count bullet; AC line 'test_configured_positive'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Consumed API contract' section that pins: `AnalyzerResult.loc: dict[str, int]  # {filepath: line_count}` (or the actual shape from ticket 3b30), so the implementer has a local spec rather than a cross-ticket dependency at implementation time.

findings.finding: The [code_health] config section with size_cap and size_near_fraction keys does not exist in the live config schema (_config_schema.py). The prerequisite ticket e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb cannot be fetched, and 'code_health' appears nowhere in _SECTION_CLASSES, _SECTIONS, or _RESERVED_SECTIONS. The plan asserts these values are 'resolved onto the context object (read from ctx)' but no ctx plumbing for a code_health section exists.
findings.location: Per-metric post-migration semantics (G6) — 'size_cap/size_near_fraction, which come from the [code_health] config section created by prerequisite [rebar:e7a0-acef-ec67-42bb], resolved onto the context object'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify the 'source' provenance label for the new module_size_distribution and oversized_module_count MetricSpec entries. The existing registry.py seed stubs for these use source='structural'; the git_metrics.py registered specs use source='git'; the new compute is backed by an scc CLI (a structural tool, not git). Leaving this unresolved risks the implementer inheriting the wrong label (e.g. keeping source='git' from the _spec helper, or keeping source='structural' from the seed stub), which affects is_authoritative() correctness and downstream signal segregation.
findings.location: Mechanism section — module_size_distribution / oversized_module_count; Files: src/rebar/metrics/git_metrics.py, src/rebar/metrics/registry.py

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
